title: Λύκος καὶ ἀρήν
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 221 (= Perry 155). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Λύκος θεασάμενος ἄρνα ἀπό τινος ποταμοῦ πίνοντα, τοῦτον ἐβουλήθη μετά τινος εὐλόγου αἰτίας καταθοινήσασθαι.
sentences.tr: Lykos theasamenos arna apo tinos potamou pinonta, touton eboulēthē meta tinos eulogou aitias katathoinēsasthai.
sentences.l: A wolf, having-caught-sight-of a lamb from a certain river drinking, him wished with some plausible charge to-make-a-feast-of.
sentences.i: A wolf saw a lamb drinking at a river, and resolved to devour him — under some plausible pretext.
sentences.n: The theme is announced in the first sentence: μετά τινος εὐλόγου αἰτίας, 'with some well-reasoned charge.' αἰτία is a legal word — accusation — and the fable is a mock-trial.

sentences.t: Διόπερ στὰς ἀνωτέρω ᾐτιᾶτο αὐτὸν ὡς θολοῦντα τὸ ὕδωρ καὶ πιεῖν αὐτὸν μὴ ἐῶντα.
sentences.tr: Dioper stas anōterō ēitiato auton hōs tholounta to hydōr kai piein auton mē eōnta.
sentences.l: Wherefore, having-stood higher-up, he-kept-accusing him as muddying the water and to-drink him not allowing.
sentences.i: So, taking his stand upstream, he accused the lamb of muddying the water and preventing him from drinking.
sentences.n: ὡς + participle (θολοῦντα … ἐῶντα) frames the charge as alleged fact: 'on the ground that he was muddying…'. The geography convicts the wolf before anyone speaks — he stands ἀνωτέρω, higher up the stream.

sentences.t: Τοῦ δὲ λέγοντος ὡς ἄκροις τοῖς χείλεσι πίνει καὶ ἄλλως οὐ δυνατὸν κατωτέρω ἑστῶτα ἐπάνω ταράσσειν τὸ ὕδωρ, ὁ λύκος ἀποτυχὼν ταύτης τῆς αἰτίας ἔφη· « Ἀλλὰ πέρυσι τὸν πατέρα μου ἐλοιδόρησας. »
sentences.tr: Tou de legontos hōs akrois tois cheilesi pinei kai allōs ou dynaton katōterō hestōta epanō tarassein to hydōr, ho lykos apotychōn tautēs tēs aitias ephē: « Alla perysi ton patera mou eloidorēsas. »
sentences.l: And he saying that with-the-tips of-the lips he-drinks, and besides not possible for-one-standing lower-down upstream to-stir the water, the wolf, having-failed of-this charge, said: « But last-year the father of-me you-reviled. »
sentences.i: The lamb answered that he drank only with the tips of his lips, and that in any case, standing downstream, he could not possibly stir the water upstream; and the wolf, his charge collapsing, said: « But last year you insulted my father. »
sentences.n: The lamb's defense is physics: κατωτέρω ἑστῶτα … ἐπάνω, 'standing lower … (stir the water) above.' ἀποτυχών + genitive, 'having failed of' — the wolf simply reaches for the next accusation.

sentences.t: Εἰπόντος δὲ ἐκείνου μηδὲ τότε γεγενῆσθαι, ὁ λύκος ἔφη πρὸς αὐτόν· « Ἐὰν σὺ ἀπολογιῶν εὐπορῇς, ἐγώ σε οὐχ ⟨ἧττον⟩ κατέδομαι. »
sentences.tr: Eipontos de ekeinou mēde tote gegenēsthai, ho lykos ephē pros auton: « Ean sy apologiōn euporēis, egō se ouch ⟨hētton⟩ katedomai. »
sentences.l: And that-one having-said not-even then to-have-been-born, the wolf said to him: « If you in-defenses abound, I you not ⟨the-less⟩ will-eat-up. »
sentences.i: The lamb replied that a year ago he had not even been born; and the wolf said to him: « Even if you never run out of defenses, I shall eat you ⟨all the same⟩. »
sentences.n: γεγενῆσθαι — 'to have been born': the unanswerable alibi, delivered in a genitive absolute. The ⟨ἧττον⟩ is Chambry's editorial supplement: οὐχ ἧττον, 'not the less' = all the same. The verdict never depended on the trial.

sentences.t: Ὁ λόγος δηλοῖ ὅτι οἷς ἡ πρόθεσίς ἐστιν ἀδικεῖν, παρ' αὐτοῖς οὐδὲ δικαία ἀπολογία ἰσχύει.
sentences.tr: Ho logos dēloi hoti hois hē prothesis estin adikein, par' autois oude dikaia apologia ischyei.
sentences.l: The story shows that for-whom the intention is to-do-wrong, with them not-even a just defense has-force.
sentences.i: The story shows that against a settled intent to do wrong, even the justest defense is powerless.
sentences.n: οἷς … παρ' αὐτοῖς — a relative clause resumed by a pronoun ('for whom… with them'), a common Koine pattern. ἀπολογία is the courtroom word for a defense speech: the mock-trial frame holds to the end.
